stories.title: The Wyvern's Ransom
stories.text: Thera the falconer rode north into the Steppe Marches seeking the wyvern Keth-Vex, whose shadow had been spotted over three villages in one week. The creature had taken a merchant's daughter—not to devour her, but to *keep* her, cradling the girl in its nest of silk and gold. When Thera found the wyvern's eyrie carved into a mountain's heart, she expected teeth and fire. Instead, Keth-Vex spoke in a voice like grinding stone: "The child carries my bloodline's debt. Her grandmother swore an oath to my sire, generations past. I merely collect what was promised." Thera realized the wyvern was not cruel—only bound by dragon-law more ancient than kingdoms. She struck a bargain: the girl for the oath-scroll hidden in the merchant's vault. Keth-Vex released the child unharmed, and Thera rode south with the merchant's gratitude and the knowledge that some treasures dragons hoard are not gold, but *promises*.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Lie
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer sold false maps to the Empire's generals for thirty years—each one beautifully rendered, entirely wrong. The Tax Collector's auditors finally caught him in a dockside tavern in Pelimor, their swords drawn. But Mardoc laughed and unrolled a real map: "I've drawn every merchant route, every pass, every river-crossing. I know this continent better than the Sovereign itself." He offered them a choice: arrest a crippled old man worth nothing, or hire him to chart the unmapped eastern reach of the Bleached Wastes—a region no kingdom had successfully explored. The Tax Collector's daughter, curious and clever, haggled on his behalf. By month's end, Mardoc led a small expedition into the white wastes, teaching the soldiers his true skill. What they found—a vast underground city of pre-human ruins—changed everything. The Empire's maps would never be the same. Mardoc's pardon was sealed in ink and wonder.

stories.title: The Widow's Vengeance
stories.text: Lady Vessa was not a widow by chance but by choice—she poisoned her husband using rare herbs administered in secret over three years. The magistrate's men fell to her blade before they understood she was armed at all. But Vessa did not flee. She walked into the great hall where the Emperor sat, knelt, and confessed everything—the poison, the murder, the slow betrayal. "I wanted him dead," she said, "and I wanted him to know I did it." The Emperor, intrigued by her honesty, offered her a counter-bargain: become his Executioner, or hang. She chose the blade. For five years she took the Empire's dirty work. When a new magistrate finally challenged her authority, Vessa smiled and said, "You may have the hall. I have the deaths." She walked free with a pension and the Emperor's respect.

stories.title: The Fae-Touched Thief
stories.text: Syleth was stolen by the Twilight Court at age seven and lived among the fae for what felt like three seasons—until she stumbled back through a moonlit grove and found a hundred years had passed. The world had forgotten her. Her family's name was dust. So Syleth became a thief, stealing not for gold but for the joy of taking what the fae had never let her touch: mortal things—bread, silk, music boxes, mirrors. When a sorcerer caught her stealing the Emerald Eyes of Vex-Khal from his spire, Syleth expected death. Instead, the sorcerer recognized the fae-mark on her wrist—the brand that bound her to the Twilight Court's whim. "They'll come for those jewels," he warned. She stole them anyway. When the fae appeared in his spire three nights later, Syleth faced them down: "I'm mortal. Your bargains don't bind me anymore." The Twilight's laughter was cold. "We'll see," they said, and vanished. The sorcerer kept the emeralds. Syleth kept running. Some debts, she learned, never truly expire—they only *wait*.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Choice
stories.text: Kess of the Tidelands sang bones back to life—not as servants, but as *witnesses*. The dead she animated spoke true, remembering their murder, their theft, their broken oaths. When the warlord Varak brought her a skeleton he claimed was his rival, Kess sang the ritual and the bones *screamed* accusations back at Varak himself: the skeleton was his own father, whom Varak had murdered for his title. Kess had no choice but to bind Varak with the dead king's own oath-magic—he would serve his father's curse or die. But Varak laughed. "You think you've punished me? You've made me *immortal*." He walked into the Tidelands and let the bog waters swallow him, half-alive, speaking prophecy to anyone mad enough to listen. Kess sang a counter-ritual to silence the bones, but it cost her voice—she became mute, hearing only the whispers of the dead. She accepted this price. Some songs, she learned, should not be sung twice.

stories.title: The Merchant's Pearl
stories.text: Torven dove for pearls in the Sapphire Deep—until the sea-kind showed him a pearl the size of a child's fist, glowing like a captured moon. "Bring us one like it," they commanded, "and we will grant you three wishes." Torven searched the ocean for a year. In the Sunken Caverns, he found a pearl guarded by the Vespera herself, a spirit-sovereign beautiful and terrible. She stood before him and said: "That pearl is my heart, given away centuries ago when I loved a mortal prince. He died. I have been alone ever since." Torven could have taken it. Instead, he asked: "What was his name?" The Vespera wept—the first tears in a thousand years—and sang his name into the darkness. Torven left the cavern empty-handed but with the Vespera's blessing. He brought news to the Tidemark Marshes, where the prince's distant descendants still lived. When the Vespera emerged from her waters to walk the shore again, she found a shrine of flowers at the tide-line, and a grave-marker carved with her prince's name. Some treasures, Torven realized, are worth more than wishes.

stories.title: The Goblin-King's Gambit
stories.text: Grethuk, Goblin-King of the Warrens beneath the Spire of Blackstone, received an ultimatum from the dwarven holds above: seal the tunnels or face invasion. The Warrens had existed for ten thousand years; sealing them meant slavery or exile. Grethuk descended alone to the deepest chamber and confronted the entity that lived in the stone itself—an ancient thing that predated both goblin and dwarf, that fed on the vibrations of footsteps and song. "What will you take," Grethuk asked, "to speak in dwarven dreams?" The entity demanded goblin memory—a thousand years of songs, genealogy, and bloodright. Grethuk paid without hesitation. The next morning, the dwarves dreamed of Warrens so vast and strange that the dwarves could not conquer them. They withdrew. The Warrens endured, but goblins forgot their own names. Grethuk wore that loss like a crown. When young goblins asked who they were, he taught them new songs from scratch—free songs, unbound by ancient debt. Some kingdoms, he realized, are worth building twice.

stories.title: The Archer's Vigil
stories.text: Ashara the Archer held the fortress of Blackthorn alone for forty days while the surrounding army waited. She was not defending a king—she was defending herself. A warlord had sworn a public oath to take the fortress or die trying. Ashara had sworn a counter-oath: she would not leave the ramparts. They were bound to each other by magic older than steel. Each dawn, the warlord sent champions. Each dusk, Ashara's arrows had sung them down—not killed, but *honored*, buried with ceremony at the fortress gates. On the thirty-ninth day, the warlord himself approached under a white banner. "You have won," he said. "I cannot break my oath, but I can fulfill it differently. Make me your sworn companion instead of your enemy." Ashara lowered her bow. "The oath binds us to enmity," she said. "Then we are bound," he agreed. The army departed.

stories.title: The Starfall Ritual
stories.text: Zareth the Astronomer-Prince climbed the Peak of Aethros on the night of the Violet Conjunction to perform the Celestial Rite—a ritual that would align the world's fate with the death and rebirth of the stars themselves. When he reached the summit, memory fled from his mind like birds from a tree. He performed the ritual anyway, on instinct alone, his hands moving through gestures he no longer understood. The stars answered. For one moment, the world held its breath—and in that breath, Zareth understood *everything*: the shape of empires yet unborn, the names of gods forgotten before kingdoms, the price the stars would demand when they died. Then the knowledge departed, leaving him blank and whole and new. At dawn, the priests found him standing on the peak, stripped of all memory but alive. They brought him down. Zareth would never know what he had done—only that the sky had *changed*, subtly, forever. The Sovereign appointed him to records, where he began again: an Astronomer with no past, charting the future with innocent hands.

stories.title: The Warband's Last Song
stories.text: Five companions—a sellsword, a bone-carver, a shadow-broker, a druid, and a dwarf—gathered at a crossroads in the Shattered West knowing this would be their final hunt together. Each had sworn a fellowship-oath twenty years before, binding them in blood and song. The oath was failing; one by one, they could feel it dissolving as they aged. They set out to find the Echoing Vaults, where legend said the oldest oaths could be *renewed*—but the price was steep: the renewal would bind them for another lifetime, unable ever to leave the other's side. In the vaults, they found not renewal but a choice: they could unbind themselves and part as friends, or bind anew and suffer together unto death. After three days of silence, the druid spoke: "Let us unbind." One by one, they cut the oath-cords tying their souls. It hurt like swords. When the last cord fell away, they wept—and laughed—and embraced as mortals, not as oath-bound. They left the vaults separately, each walking a different road. The bond remained, but gentler now: *chosen*, not *compelled*. Some fellowships, they learned, are strongest when they are free.
